content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 5, 1998


Russell H. Miller
Attorney at Law
20 Park Road, Suite E
Burlingame, California 94010-4443

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-98-229

Dear Mr. Miller:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Santa Clara County
Supervisor S. Joseph Simitian regarding the "personal use" provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

May Supervisor Simitian use his campaign funds to attend a master's degree program at
Stanford University, which is designed to provide intensive training in international aspects of
public policy, business, political science and the economy?

CONCLUSION

The expenditure is not directly related to a legislative, governmental or political purpose.
Therefore, the supervisor may not use his campaign funds to attend the program.

FACTS

Supervisor Simitian plans to attend a master's degree program in International Policy
Studies at Stanford University. This graduate program is specifically designed to provide
graduate students with intensive training in international aspects of public policy, business,

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-98-229
Page 2

political science, and the economy, and to assist them as decisionmakers in governmental
positions. It is an interdepartmental course of study, focusing on both analytical skills and
substantive knowledge, designed to expose the participants to a variety of Stanford scholars and
institutions, including the Institute for International Studies and the Hoover Institution. You
have attached materials describing the program and its courses.

The classes in the program include: "Introduction to Political Data Analysis," "Strategy
in Politics," "Economics and Public Policy," "Issues in International Economics," "Decision
Making in U.S. Foreign Policy," "Ethnic and National Identities," "Technology, Policy &
Management in New-Industrial Countries" and "Environmental Policy Design &
Implementation."

Currently, Santa Clara County faces the following issues:

1. Understanding Implications of the County's International Population

The county has a large and diverse immigrant population, including more Bosnian
refugees than any other county in Northern California, as well as citizens from a vast array of
ethnic and cultural backgrounds. Recent census data show that of the county's almost 1.7
million residents, more than 400,000 residents are immigrants. Supervisor Simitian was a United
States government observer at the time of general elections in Bosnia, and is currently making a
return trip.

2. Developing Programs Targeting the Needs of Refugees and Immigrants

The county has spent more than $250,000 to supplement the efforts of the Immigration
and Naturalization Service ("INS") in expediting citizenship of legal immigrants. In August
1998, the county loaned ten county staff members to the INS to assist with the processing of
immigrants. The Santa Clara Valley Health & Hospital System, which is funded and operated by
the county, developed the Refugee and Child Health Program to address the needs of immigrants.
In addition, the county made efforts to fund programs addressing the special mental health needs
of refugee populations, such as the Culturally Specific Family Services Program of its mental
health department.

Other groups that are organized to help immigrants and refugees request funding from the
county. For example, Jewish Vocational Services from San Francisco opened a satellite office in
the county and is seeking county funding. Its purpose is to serve the large Russian emigre
population in the county by providing welfare and job-training services.

File No. A-98-229
Page 3

3. Understanding the County's Position in the Global Economy

The thriving economic and business conditions of the county have a strong international
component that present challenges and opportunities for the county. Recently, in a successful
effort to secure a higher bond rating for the county, Supervisor Simitian appeared before
Standard & Poor's to explain the central role Silicon Valley plays in the global economy and to
address questions concerning the effect of worldwide economic conditions, such as the Asian
economic crises, on the county. The success of his presentation and handling of questions
combined with Standard & Poor's increased confidence in the county's money management,
resulted in an upgraded bond rating for the county and a corresponding substantial savings in the
amount of interest the county will have to pay to finance projects. While he was successful in
this particular situation, it reconfirmed for Supervisor Simitian the value of a greater knowledge
of international economic conditions.

Supervisor Simitian maintains a campaign account "Friends of Simitian" (ID # 980570),
which he has designated as his single bank account under section 85201. He intends to use funds
in this account to pay for his tuition. The supervisor believes that participation in the graduate
program will provide him with essential knowledge and skills that are directly related to his
legislative and governmental work. As described above, governing Santa Clara County presents
a host of complicated issues, based on or influenced by international conditions. The board must
develop carefully crafted policies, programs and ordinances that reflect an understanding of
matters as diverse as international finance and the global economy on the one hand, and ethnic
tensions and the needs of refugees living in the county on the other. Therefore, even though
participation in the program may confer a substantial personal benefit, you believe that
participation in the program is a permissible use of campaign funds because it is an expenditure
associated with holding office that is directly related to a legislative or governmental purpose.

Further, you believe that the graduate program is an expenditure that is directly related to
a political purpose. The supervisor's district has one of the most highly educated electorates in
the world, and you believe that such voters gravitate to candidates who reflect their own
educational level, skills and knowledge. In addition, it is your opinion that the importance of
Stanford University to the voters in the district cannot be underestimated. You believe that a
candidate who holds a degree from Stanford has an important political asset that goes beyond
mere educational attainment, but entailing as well an appreciation and understanding of the
uniqueness and value of Stanford to the county's continued success.

ANALYSIS

The "personal use" provisions of the Act are designed to prevent candidates from
benefitting privately from their campaign activities. The fundamental purpose of the personal
use provisions is "to ensure that candidates, elected officers and the people clearly recognize

File No. A-98-229
Page 4

acceptable and unacceptable uses of campaign funds." (Senate Elections Committee, Analysis of
Senate Bill 1431, June 7, 1989.) Therefore, all contributions deposited into a candidate's
campaign account are deemed to be held in trust for expenses associated with the election of the
candidate to office or expenses associated with holding that office. (Section 89510(b).)

An expenditure is within the lawful execution of that trust if it is reasonably related to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose. However, if an expenditure confers a substantial
personal benefit on a candidate, it must be directly related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose. (Section 89512.) A "substantial personal benefit" means an expenditure
of campaign funds which results in a direct personal benefit with a value of more than $100 to
the candidate. (Section 89511(b)(3).) An expenditure of campaign funds results in a "direct
personal benefit" when, within six months of the expenditure and without the assistance of an
intervening influence or interruption, the candidate: 1) realizes an increase in his or her income
or assets, or a decrease in his or her expenses or liabilities, of more than $100 from the
expenditure; or 2) actually makes personal use of an asset obtained as a result of the expenditure.
(Regulation 18960.)

We have advised that an officeholder may expend campaign funds for educational
development that is specifically designed to improve the official's performance of his or her
official duties. (Braly Advice Letter, No. A-90-596.) To illustrate, we have advised that an
officeholder could use campaign moneys for the following types of instructional activities:
computer training the official used to perform governmental duties; video image training
intended to promote effective communication during televised city council meetings; a three-
week program offered by Harvard University designed to improve the problem-solving and
administrative skills of state and local officials; a conference in Hawaii for school board
members regarding overcrowding of schools; a conference in Arizona for school board members
concerning bilingual education; and an annual convention for legislators from various states.
(Weintraub Advice Letter, No. A-90-210; Murphy Advice Letter, No. A-90-301; Walters Advice
Letter, No. A-90-308; Neveau Advice Letter, No. I-90-595; Diaz Advice Letter, No. A-94-020;
Kaloogian Advice Letter, No. A-97-111.)

A candidate may also spend campaign funds for educational programs that are directly
related to a political purpose. An expenditure of campaign funds is directly related to a political
purpose when the payment is necessary to the quest for elective office and is incurred in
connection with activities designed to enhance the candidate's visibility in the community and to
inform voters of the candidate's positions on issues relevant to his or her campaign for elective
office. (Tucker Advice Letter, No. A-91-418.) For example, we advised a candidate for city
council that she could use her campaign funds to pay for a training program cosponsored by the
Women's Studies Program at Yale University, which was designed to assist women entering the
political process. (Calandra Advice Letter, No. A-94-132.)

File No. A-98-229
Page 5

Upon completing the graduate program, Supervisor Simitian will have a master's degree
in International Policy Studies. According to the brochure enclosed with your letter, graduates of
the program include law partners, top-ranking governmental officials, CEOs of startups
companies, consultants, writers, journalists and executives with Fortune 500 and multinational
companies. Thus, the personal benefit the supervisor will receive from the program will be more
than just the knowledge he will gain from his participation. He will be achieving a graduate
degree, a credential that will open up opportunities for the supervisor in several diverse non-
governmental fields.

Clearly the information the supervisor will learn from the program is pertinent to his
present position as a county supervisor. However, the relevant inquiry is whether the academic
degree, itself, is directly related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose. An academic
degree is similar to a professional license. In the past, we have followed the Attorney General's
approach regarding the expenditure of campaign moneys for professional licenses.² In an
advisory letter to a legislator, the Attorney General's Office opined:

"Although we recognize that the holding of a professional license
by a candidate or officeholder may bestow additional prestige on
him or her, we think that the benefits of holding a professional
license are essentially personal rather than political, legislative or
governmental. Accordingly, for the reasonable relationship test to
be satisfied, we think a benefit beyond that of prestige or notoriety
from the mere holding of the license must flow to the candidate,
the campaign committee, or a governmental entity." (Letter to
Assemblymember Phillip D. Wyman, Attorney General Advisory
Letter, No. 88-052, dated May 3, 1988.)

The letter proceeded to advise that the payment of bar dues with campaign funds was
permissible under two conditions. First, the legislator must be obligated to perform the role of
legal counsel in connection with a political, legislative or governmental purpose. Second, the
legislator could not have a private law practice.

Accordingly, campaign funds may be used for an academic degree or professional license
if it is necessary for the official to have the degree or license in order to carry out his or her
official duties. Since it is not essential for the supervisor to have a master's degree in
International Policy Studies in order to exercise his duties as a member of the Santa Clara
County Board of Supervisors, we conclude that the graduate program is not directly related to a
political, legislative or governmental purpose.

² The expenditure of campaign funds was formerly governed by the Elections Code as interpreted by the
Attorney General's Office.

File No. A-98-229
Page 6

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Julia Butcher
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

QUESTION: May Supervisor Simitian use his campaign funds to attend a master’s degree program at
Stanford University, which is designed to provide intensive training in international aspects of
public policy, business, political science and the economy?

CONCLUSION: The expenditure is not directly related to a legislative, governmental or political purpose.
Therefore, the supervisor may not use his campaign funds to attend the program.
